source_metadata.title_raw: Charles B. Christensen - A-19-097 - July 15, 2019 - San Diego

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION ·
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 15, 2019

Charles B. Christensen
General Counsel
550 West C Street, Suite 1660
San Diego, CA 92101

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-19-097

Dear Mr. Christensen:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the San Diego Housing
Commission and Commissioner Timothy Walsh regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090, not under other general conflict
of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.

     Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

     In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (See Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

                                          QUESTION

     Does Section 1090 prohibit the San Diego Housing Commission from recommending the
San Diego City Council from approving the issuance of bonds where Commissioner Walsh's
potential employer will perform work financed by the bond?

                                        CONCLUSION

     No. An advisory board member, Commissioner Walsh's potential employment will not
result in a violation of Section 1090 in this circumstance as long as he continues to recuse himself
from any decisions related to his proposed new employer.


_________________________________
¹ All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-097
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

The San Diego Housing Commission provides advice and recommendations to the Housing
Authority of the City of San Diego ("Housing Authority"), a body corporate and politic. The San
Diego Housing Commission ("Housing Commission") does not have authority to take final actions
on the approval of the issuance of Multifamily Housing bonds ("Bonds"), that are issued under the
provisions of the Housing Authority law to assist households earning at or below 60% of the Area
Median Income ("AMI"). All Housing Commission actions concerning these Bonds are only
advisory to the Housing Authority and the City Council of the City of San Diego. The Housing
Authority approves bond inducements and final issuance of the bonds. The City Council holds a
Tax Equity and Fiscal Responsibility Act ("TEFRA") hearing in connection with the issuance of the
Bonds. All bond issuances require the approval of the Housing Authority as well as the City
Council.

There are currently seven Commissioners on the Housing Commission Board.
Commissioner Walsh is not compensated for his service on the board of this public agency, and is
neither a board member of the Housing Authority nor a City Councilmember.

Commissioner Walsh is currently employed by the federal government, but is now
considering employment with C&C Development Group, LLC ("C&C"), a real estate development
company. The Principals of C&C are Casey Healing and Colin Rice. Commissioner Walsh has
known Mr. Rice for many years, and had a business dealing with him and his company several
years ago. He has no current business dealings with Mr. Rice or any of the developer entities
associated with the project. C&C is one of the companies that would be working on the
rehabilitation of Luther Tower and Wesley Terrace, two projects that are being partially funded
through Multifamily Housing bonds.

First Bond Transaction-Luther Tower

In the "First Bond Transaction," a group of corporations and limited partnerships including
C&C were the subject of a proposal to authorize the issuance of up to $20,000,000 in Multifamily
Housing Revenue Bonds, to facilitate the acquisition and rehabilitation of Luther Tower, a 200-unit
affordable rental housing development. You note that Commissioner Walsh was absent during an
initial meeting when the Housing Commission discussed this proposal, and that he recused himself
when the Housing Commission voted on the recommendation.

Second Bond Transaction- Wesley Terrace

Recently, the corporations and limited partnerships, including C&C, sought additional
Bonds for another project ("Second Bond Transaction"), which would involve the acquisition and
rehabilitation of Wesley Terrace, an existing 161-unit affordable senior rental housing development.

Wesley Terrace is approximately 46 years old, and the building is in need of capital
improvements. The developer is requesting the issuance of up to $22,000,000 in Multifamily
Housing Revenue Bonds to finance the acquisition and rehabilitation of the property to extend its
useful life and maintain its marketability. The scope of the proposed rehabilitation includes

File No. A-19-097
Page No. 3

comprehensive improvements to unit interiors, common areas and elevator, as well as
improvements to building electrical, plumbing, and mechanical systems.

In the Second Bond Transaction, the Housing Commission took no action for lack of a
quorum. The Second Bond Transaction related matters were referred to the City Council and
Housing Authority for action without any recommendation from the Housing Commission.

You also note that Commissioner Walsh had no discussions with the principals concerning
the proposed projects. All bond issuances require the approval of the Housing Authority as well as
the City Council so the bonds could not have been legally issued without the approval of these two
entities.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.)

Notably, when members of a public board, commission or similar body have the power to
execute contracts, each member is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of all
contracts by his or her agency regardless of whether the member actually participates in the making
of the contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra at pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of
Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).)

With respect to the making of a contract, Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who
participate personally in the actual execution of the contract to capture those officials who
participate in any way in the making of the contract. (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046,
1052.) Therefore, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and
specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.)

Here, we must consider whether Commissioner Walsh has a financial interest in C&C as a
potential employer. We must also consider whether the Housing Commission can recommend, and
the City Council can approve, the issuance of bonds should Commissioner Walsh accept
employment with a company where he will then work on a project funded under that Bond.
We must also consider whether the Housing Commission can recommend, and the City Council can approve, the issuance of bonds should Commissioner Walsh accept employment with a company where he will then work on a project funded under that Bond.

File No. A-19-097
Page No. 4

Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).)

Furthermore, case law and statutory exceptions to Section 1090 make clear that the term "financially interested" must be liberally interpreted. (See, e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146 ("[h]owever devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void"].) The phrase "financially interested" broadly encompasses anything that would tie a public official's fortunes to the existence of a public contract. (Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla, supra, at p. 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations omitted).) As such, Commissioner Walsh likely has an interest in C&C under section 1090, as he has been in the process of discussing prospective employment.

Despite likely having a financial interest, Section 1090 does not apply in these circumstances because the Housing Commission is an advisory board. Neither the Attorney General nor any court has ever "extended the proscription against making a contract by a board with a financially interested member to the situation of an advisory committee with a financially interested member." (82 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126, 130 (1999).) "Rather, an advisory committee may perform its responsibilities as long as the interested member abstains and does not participate in the giving of advice." (Ibid.; see also Ueda Advice Letter, No. A-16-104.) "They are treated differently from the boards responsible for executing the actual contracts under the governing statutes and policy reasons for the prohibition. Abstention by the interested member is allowed for the one, but not for the other." (Ibid.)

Here, Commissioner Walsh did not participate in the Housing Commission's recommendation of the First Bond Transaction so he would not have a prohibitive financial interest in that agreement that would preclude the Housing Commission from making a recommendation on bond funding. So long as he recuses himself from participating in the making of a contract related to the Project, if he accepts employment with one of the entities seeking bond funding, the Housing Commission is not prohibited from doing so, including making recommendations to the Housing Authority and the City Council.

File No. A-19-097
Page No. 5

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja
